overview: The largemouth bass is Ontario's most popular sport fish. Recognizable by the large mouth that extends past the eye and a distinct lateral line break. Thrives in warm, weedy lakes and slow rivers.
size_range: Typical 30–50 cm, max 70 cm. Ontario record ~4 kg.
ontario_distribution: Southern Ontario lakes and rivers. Most abundant in the Kawartha Lakes, Rideau system, Lake Erie shoreline, and Lake Simcoe.

habitat.notes: Ambush predator. Rarely found in cold, deep water. Prefers sheltered bays with ample aquatic vegetation.

feeding.spring: Pre-spawn fish move shallow and feed aggressively. Target staging areas near spawning flats in 16–20°C water. Best in mid-May to late June.
feeding.summer: Peak feeding during solunar windows, dawn and dusk. Fish move to edges of weed beds and shade structures. Top water bite in calm, warm evenings.
feeding.fall: Feeding increases before water temps drop below 15°C. Fish follow baitfish into open water then return to structure. Best September to mid-October.
feeding.winter: Lethargic and rarely caught through ice. Deep basin edges at 6–10m. Slow, finesse presentations only.

techniques.name: Texas-rigged soft plastic
techniques.description: Weedless rigging allows fishing through thick vegetation. Creature baits and stick worms in natural colours work year-round.
techniques.best_conditions: Overcast skies, moderate wind, stable to falling pressure

techniques.name: Top water (poppers / frogs)
techniques.description: Most effective at dawn and dusk over weed beds. Surface disturbance triggers instinctive strikes.
techniques.best_conditions: Calm water, low light, warm nights, stable pressure

techniques.name: Jig and trailer
techniques.description: Football or flipping jig with crawfish trailer. Slow drag along bottom near structure.
techniques.best_conditions: Clear water, slow-falling pressure, cool mornings

techniques.name: Spinnerbait
techniques.description: Effective along weed edges and around timber. Willowleaf blades in clear water, Colorado blades in stained water.
techniques.best_conditions: Overcast, slight chop, moderate wind

overview: Often called the 'gamest fish that swims,' smallmouth bass are renowned for their acrobatic fights. Bronze-coloured with vertical bars. Prefer clear, cool lakes and fast rivers.
size_range: Typical 30–45 cm, max 60 cm. Ontario record ~3 kg.
ontario_distribution: Clear, rocky lakes across Ontario from southern shield to Georgian Bay. Abundant in the French River, Lake St. Clair, and Lake Ontario near shore.

habitat.notes: Prefers cleaner, cooler water than largemouth. Found in current in rivers. Rocky substrate is key.

feeding.spring: Spawn on gravel in 12–18°C water, 2–5m depth. Males guard nests aggressively. Pre-spawn bite is excellent in May.
feeding.summer: Crayfish are the primary forage on lakes. Bait schools of perch and shiners offshore. Feed heavily during solunar windows.
feeding.fall: Outstanding fall bite as fish bulk up before ice. Target rock humps and points. Can be caught until ice-up.
feeding.winter: Lethargic but catchable through ice on live bait near rocky structure.

techniques.name: Tube jig
techniques.description: Finesse presentation that mimics crayfish. Drag slowly along rocky bottom. Natural brown and green colours best.
techniques.best_conditions: Clear water, sunny days, stable pressure

techniques.name: Drop shot
techniques.description: Vertical presentation for finicky fish. Use small finesse worms 10–30cm above weight. Excellent for suspended fish.
techniques.best_conditions: Post-cold-front, high pressure, clear skies

techniques.name: Ned rig
techniques.description: Ultra-light mushroom head jig with small stick worm. Stands up on bottom and triggers neutral fish.
techniques.best_conditions: Tough conditions, clear water, bright days

techniques.name: Swimbait / paddle tail
techniques.description: Mimics shad or cisco forage. Cast along rocky points and retrieve steadily at 1–3m depth.
techniques.best_conditions: Low light, overcast, active baitfish visible on sonar

overview: Ontario's most sought-after table fish. Named for its light-reflecting eyes adapted for low-light feeding. Olive-gold colouring with a white-tipped lower tail fin.
size_range: Typical 40–65 cm, max 90 cm. Ontario record ~10 kg.
ontario_distribution: Widespread across Ontario in clear to moderately turbid lakes and rivers. Exceptional fisheries in Lake Erie, Lake Simcoe, Lake Nipissing, and the Kawartha Lakes.

habitat.notes: Highly light-sensitive. Most active at dawn, dusk, and on overcast days. Moves deep on bright sunny days.

feeding.spring: Spawn on rocky shoals in 6–8°C water in April–May. Best fishing starts when water reaches 12°C. Shallow at night, deeper in day.
feeding.summer: Moves to thermocline depth in warm weather. Dawn/dusk pattern strongest. Overcast days produce all-day bites.
feeding.fall: Excellent fall bite as fish feed aggressively before freeze-up. Best September to November on shallow rocky structure.
feeding.winter: Top ice fishing species. Fish suspended over deep basins and at weed edges. Jigging in 6–12m typical.

techniques.name: Jig and live minnow
techniques.description: Walleye's most reliable presentation. 1/8–1/2 oz jig with a lively minnow. Bounced along bottom.
techniques.best_conditions: Low light, cloudy, dawn or dusk, solunar windows

techniques.name: Trolling with harness
techniques.description: Worm harness trolled at 1–1.5 km/h along depth contours. Locates active fish over large areas.
techniques.best_conditions: Stable or rising pressure, overcast, light wind

techniques.name: Slip float with minnow
techniques.description: Suspend minnow at precise depth near structure. Set float to keep minnow 15–30cm off bottom.
techniques.best_conditions: Mild current, dawn feeding period, spring/fall

overview: Apex predator of Ontario's north. Torpedo-shaped with distinctive bean-shaped light spots on a dark green body. An aggressive, year-round target species.
size_range: Typical 55–90 cm, max 130+ cm. Ontario record ~15 kg.
ontario_distribution: Ubiquitous throughout Ontario's lakes and rivers. Largest specimens from remote northern lakes, Lake of the Woods, and Lake Nipissing.

habitat.notes: Prefers cooler water than bass. Retreats deeper in summer heat. Ambush predator that waits motionless in vegetation.

feeding.spring: Most aggressive feeding period. Fish shallow bays immediately after ice-out while water is still cold. Large pike active all day.
feeding.summer: Retreats to deeper, cooler water in heat of day. Best fishing at dawn in emergent vegetation. Overcast days extend active periods.
feeding.fall: Feeding intensifies as water cools. Trophy-sized fish caught in October and November. Best period for large fish.
feeding.winter: Active through ice. Fish over submerged weed beds in 2–5m. Live and dead bait both effective.

techniques.name: Large spinnerbait or bucktail
techniques.description: Bulky presentations with strong vibration. Retrieve through weed edges. Use wire leader to prevent bite-offs.
techniques.best_conditions: Overcast, moderate wind, stained water

techniques.name: Sucker or ciscos (live bait)
techniques.description: Large live bait under a bobber or on a set line. Most effective for trophy fish. Use large circle hook.
techniques.best_conditions: Slow pressure days, early morning, pre-front

techniques.name: Large surface lures
techniques.description: Prop baits and glide baits retrieved over weedy bays at dawn. Spectacular surface strikes.
techniques.best_conditions: Calm water, low light, humid mornings

overview: The 'fish of ten thousand casts' — Ontario's largest freshwater predator and the most coveted freshwater trophy. Greenish with dark vertical bars or spots, massive jaws.
size_range: Typical 80–120 cm, max 150+ cm. Ontario record ~32 kg.
ontario_distribution: Selected lakes and rivers — not ubiquitous. Notable muskie waters include Lake St. Clair, French River, Lake of Bays, St. Lawrence River, Lac Seul, and Georgian Bay.

habitat.notes: Solitary apex predator with large territories. 'Figure-8' at the boat — follow-through every cast as muskies often follow lures and strike boat-side.

feeding.spring: Post-spawn recovery until mid-July. Smaller baits more effective during early season. Fish are present but not aggressive.
feeding.summer: Peak feeding activity. Best windows are early morning, evening, and overcast days. Feeding binges last minutes — timing is critical.
feeding.fall: Best season for trophy fish. Cold water triggers binge feeding to fatten up. October is considered peak muskie month in Ontario.
feeding.winter: Season typically closed. Fish become very lethargic.

techniques.name: Large glide bait / swimbait
techniques.description: 9–14 inch S-shaped swimbaits mimic large baitfish. Slow, wide-sweep retrieve. Premium option for trophy fish.
techniques.best_conditions: Stable or slowly falling pressure, overcast, solunar peak

techniques.name: Bucktail spinner
techniques.description: Classic muskie presentation. Large Colorado blade with full bucktail. Steady retrieve just under the surface.
techniques.best_conditions: Overcast with wind chop, moderate temperature, dawn and dusk

techniques.name: Large jerkbait (Suick, Believer)
techniques.description: Twitch-pause presentation for cold-water muskies. Fish hang deeper and respond to paused, dying-bait action.
techniques.best_conditions: Water below 15°C, fall turnover, overcast days

overview: Ontario's cold-water icon. Deeply forked tail, worm-like light spots on dark grey-green body. Requires cold, well-oxygenated water. Found only in quality lakes.
size_range: Typical 45–75 cm, max 100+ cm. Ontario record ~29 kg.
ontario_distribution: Deep, cold, clean Canadian Shield lakes. Best fisheries: Lake Superior, Lake Huron, Georgian Bay, Temagami area, and countless remote northern lakes.

habitat.notes: Stays in cold water year-round. Moves shallow in spring and fall when surface temps cool. Requires dissolved oxygen — won't tolerate stratified warm-water lakes.

feeding.spring: Best surface/shallow period immediately after ice-out. Fish rocky points and shoals in 3–6m. Highly aggressive and accessible.
feeding.summer: Retreats to cold depths following the thermocline (typically 15–30m). Requires downriggers or deep jigging techniques.
feeding.fall: Spawns on rocky shoals in October–November. Comes shallow again. Exceptional shallow fishing before and during spawn.
feeding.winter: Active and accessible through ice. Fish suspended at the thermocline or over deep structure. Best January–March.

techniques.name: Tube jig or bucktail (spring/fall)
techniques.description: Cast and slow-roll along rocky structure when lakers are shallow. White, silver, and orange are effective colours.
techniques.best_conditions: Cold water (below 12°C), early season, early morning

techniques.name: Jigging spoon (deep summer)
techniques.description: Heavy spoons jigged vertically at depth over known structure. Required when fish are 15–35m down.
techniques.best_conditions: Stable weather, mid-day when fish are holding deep

techniques.name: Trolling with flashers
techniques.description: Cowbells or flasher-fly rig trolled at depth on a downrigger. Covers water and finds suspended fish.
techniques.best_conditions: Clear water, sunny days when fish are deep

overview: Rainbow trout that migrate between Lake Ontario and its tributaries are called steelhead — silver sea-run fish averaging 2–5 kg. Resident lake rainbows are smaller and pink-striped.
size_range: Lake resident: 30–50 cm. Steelhead: 50–80 cm, max 90+ cm.
ontario_distribution: Lake Ontario tributaries (Credit, Humber, Ganaraska, Saugeen) for steelhead runs. Lake Superior and northern inland lakes for resident fish.

habitat.notes: Steelhead migrate into rivers twice yearly: fall run (October–November) and spring run (March–May). Fish most accessible in rivers.

feeding.spring: Spring steelhead run — fish enter tributaries to spawn in March–May. Aggressive feeders on spawn sacs, nymphs, and streamers.
feeding.summer: Resident lake fish active in cold tributary pools. Best in northern lakes and streams. Avoid warm water periods.
feeding.fall: Fall steelhead run begins in October. Fresh-run fish are bright silver and extremely strong. Best fishing in freshets.
feeding.winter: Steelhead can be caught in rivers through winter in southern Ontario tributaries. Slow nymphing in deep pools.

techniques.name: Float fishing with spawn sac
techniques.description: Drift spawn sacs or beads under a float at current speed through holding pools. Dominant presentation for steelhead.
techniques.best_conditions: Rising or stable water levels, overcast, fresh-run fish

techniques.name: Spoon or inline spinner
techniques.description: Cast and retrieve through pools and tailouts. Silver and chartreuse most effective on fresh fish.
techniques.best_conditions: Clear water, fish recently arrived in river, morning light

techniques.name: Jig and float
techniques.description: Small tube or marabou jig drifted under a float at depth. Excellent for finicky fish in clear water.
techniques.best_conditions: High pressure post-front, clear water, slow current

overview: Introduced from Europe, brown trout have thrived in Ontario's cold rivers and lake tributaries. Golden-brown body with black and red spots encircled in white halos. Known for being wary and selective.
size_range: Typical 35–60 cm, max 80+ cm. Trophy fish over 5 kg exist in Lake Ontario tributaries.
ontario_distribution: Lake Ontario tributaries (Credit, Ganaraska, Nottawasaga, Saugeen), Lake Simcoe area streams, and scattered Ontario rivers with cold-water inputs.

habitat.notes: Most wary of Ontario's trout species. Feeds actively at night. Prefers specific holding spots and can be patterned.

feeding.spring: Active surface and subsurface feeding in cool spring conditions. Hatch feeding common. Excellent April–June on rivers.
feeding.summer: Moves to cold springs and deep pools in heat. Best fishing in early morning or evening. Nocturnal surface activity.
feeding.fall: Spawning run in October–November. Fish stage in lake near tributary mouths then move upstream. Strong, dark-coloured fall fish.
feeding.winter: Some river fishing in southern Ontario during mild spells. Slow presentations in deep pools near bottom.

techniques.name: Dry fly (hatch matching)
techniques.description: Surface feeding during mayfly, caddis, and stonefly hatches. Precise presentation with tapered leader. Most technical presentation.
techniques.best_conditions: Active hatch, overcast, calm water surface, evening

techniques.name: Streamer / sculpin pattern
techniques.description: Large minnow-imitating flies stripped through pools. Triggers large predatory fish. Strip fast past structure.
techniques.best_conditions: Overcast, rising water, aggressive fish, low light

techniques.name: Inline spinner (Mepps, Worden's)
techniques.description: Small to medium spinners cast upstream and retrieved at current speed through pools and riffles.
techniques.best_conditions: Morning, overcast, moderate current, stable pressure

techniques.name: Wobbling spoon at night
techniques.description: Brown trout feed actively at night in summer. Small wobbling spoons in silver or black retrieve slowly over known holding spots.
techniques.best_conditions: Warm nights, low light, stable weather
